conversation.title: Sam Harris vs. Ben Shapiro: The 2024 Choice
conversation.main_topic: A debate between Sam Harris and Ben Shapiro regarding the qualifications and dangers of Donald Trump versus Kamala Harris ahead of the 2024 election.

topics.label: Introduction and Context
topics.description: Opening remarks setting the stage for the debate.

topics.label: Candidate Endorsements
topics.description: The primary reasons for supporting Harris or Trump.

topics.label: Ben Shapiro's Political Evolution
topics.description: Discussion of Shapiro's shift from Never-Trump to supporting Trump.

topics.label: Character and Fitness
topics.description: The moral and personal attributes of the candidates.

topics.label: January 6th and Transfer of Power
topics.description: The events of the 2020 election and the peaceful transfer of power.

topics.label: Policy and Governance
topics.description: Specific policy proposals and the functionality of the political system.

nodes.speaker: Bari Weiss
nodes.text: From the Free Press. This is honestly, and I'm Bari Weiss. There are no perfect political candidates. But what do you do when both candidates are not just imperfect but deeply flawed and, according to half of the country, unqualified?

nodes.speaker: Bari Weiss
nodes.text: We love this guy. He says, you're not going to be a dictator, are you? I said, no, no, no. Other than day one, we're closing the border.

nodes.speaker: Bari Weiss
nodes.text: You were a very staunch defender of President Biden's capacity to serve another four years. Right after the debate, you insist President Biden is extraordinarily strong.

nodes.speaker: Bari Weiss
nodes.text: Given where we are now, do you have any regrets about what you told the American people? No, not at all. Not at all. I have.

nodes.speaker: Bari Weiss
nodes.text: In Springfield, they're eating the dogs. The people that came in, they're eating the cats. They're eating. They're eating the pets of the people that live there.

nodes.speaker: Bari Weiss
nodes.text: Well, there was a lot that was done, but there's more to do, Anderson. And I'm pointing out things that need to be done that haven't been done. But need to be done.

nodes.speaker: Bari Weiss
nodes.text: We did great in 2016. A lot of people don't know. We did much better in 2020. We won. We won. We did win. It was a rigged election. It was a rigged election. Is there something you can point to in your life, political life, or in your life in the last four years that you think is a mistake that you have learned from?

nodes.speaker: Bari Weiss
nodes.text: I mean, I've, I've made many mistakes, and they range from, you know, if you've ever parented a child, you know, you make lots of mistakes, too. In my role as vice president, I mean, I've probably worked very hard at making sure that I am well versed on issues. And I think that is very important. It's a mistake not to be well versed on an issue and feel compelled to answer a question.

nodes.speaker: Bari Weiss
nodes.text: We are just one week away from a presidential election that will decide if the next four years are helmed by Vice President Kamala Harris or former President Donald Trump. I know a lot of people who are still undecided. Some of them work here at the Free Press.

nodes.speaker: Bari Weiss
nodes.text: These undecided voters have just one presidential debate to reference. And as my friends at open debates said in a Wall Street Journal op ed recently, I can confidently state that we haven't yet seen a real presidential debate this year.

nodes.speaker: Bari Weiss
nodes.text: Debates have devolved into political theater with combative candidates, biased media agenda driven moderators, and a fixation on social media sound bites. This structure fails to deliver the substance voters need.

nodes.speaker: Bari Weiss
nodes.text: So today I'm here without the pageantry the makeup or the muted mics to host. Not Trump vs Kamala, though the invitation, if you guys are watching, is still very much open. But two very smart people who represent each side of the choice that we are going to make a week from today.

nodes.speaker: Bari Weiss
nodes.text: Sam Harris, unrelated to Kamala Harris, is a neuroscientist, philosopher, bestselling author, and host of the essential podcast Making Sense. Today he will explain why he is voting for Harris.

nodes.speaker: Bari Weiss
nodes.text: Sam has spoken passionately and consistently on this issue since Trump came onto the scene. He calls Trump, quote, the most dangerous cult leader on earth and highlights Trump's character flaws.

nodes.speaker: Bari Weiss
nodes.text: On the other side, Ben Shapiro. Ben is a lawyer, the co founder of the Daily Wire, a bestselling author like Sam, and host of the Ben Shapiro Show. He'll explain why he's voting for Donald Trump.

nodes.speaker: Sam Harris
nodes.text: Well, I think it's no secret at this point that I'm not a fan of Donald Trump's and I'm voting for Harris. The truth is I would vote for almost anyone over Trump.

nodes.speaker: Sam Harris
nodes.text: So a lot of this conversation is going to be talking about why I think Trump has disqualified himself for the role of president and I'm happy to talk about Harris as well, but that really is the whole story for me.

nodes.speaker: Sam Harris
nodes.text: I would be voting against Trump with more or less anyone because what I want really is normal politics. Right? The fact that politics has taken up so much of our bandwidth of late, for really, for eight years, nine years at this point is a sign that something's wrong, right?

nodes.speaker: Sam Harris
nodes.text: And Trump is at the very center of that. So nothing I say in this conversation is going to come out of a feeling of my own partisanship. You know, I happen to be registered as a Democrat, but I would vote for Mitt Romney at this point.

nodes.speaker: Sam Harris
nodes.text: I don't think any of this gets better until Trump gets out of politics.

nodes.speaker: Ben Shapiro
nodes.text: So obviously I'm coming at this from precisely the opposite point of view. I've given money to President Trump, I've campaigned with President Trump.

nodes.speaker: Ben Shapiro
nodes.text: The question is, were you better off in 2019 or are you better off in 2024? And the way that I view this political race is that basically you have this strainer that is American politics.

nodes.speaker: Ben Shapiro
nodes.text: After the strainer in 2019, what I got was a solid economy, the best foreign policy of my lifetime, a president whose rhetoric I very often didn't like, but who had made the country stronger in a variety of ways.

nodes.speaker: Ben Shapiro
nodes.text: Donald Trump was a better president than Kamala Harris would be. With all Donald Trump's flaws, with all of his excesses, and because we do have a very robust and durable constitutional system almost built to hem in his flaws and excesses.

nodes.speaker: Bari Weiss
nodes.text: Ben, you've moved a lot since 2016. In 2016, you wrote a column titled I will never vote for Donald Trump. I want to just read a little bit of that back to you.

nodes.speaker: Bari Weiss
nodes.text: I will never vote for Donald Trump because I stand with certain principles. I stand with small government and free markets and religious freedom and personal responsibility. Donald Trump, you wrote, stands against all of these things. What changed in these eight years for you?

nodes.speaker: Ben Shapiro
nodes.text: Sure, he was president. I mean, the thing that changed is, as you can read there, there are a number of things that I assumed his presidency would be. And in 2015, 2016, he was, to be fair to me, incredibly unclear about what exactly his policies would be.

nodes.speaker: Ben Shapiro
nodes.text: And then he was president. And then I could base my next vote on what he would be like as president because he actually was president. He appointed justices to the Supreme Court who I generally thought were quite good.

nodes.speaker: Ben Shapiro
nodes.text: One of the big questions that I have for folks who sort of see Donald Trump as this Hitlerian Mussolini esque figure is if that's true, then why didn't we see that in term one, he tried to do many things. Those things were generally unsuccessful in terms of his excesses.

nodes.speaker: Sam Harris
nodes.text: Well, as Ben said himself, he was checked by people around him. Right. And the concern now is that he would have not General Milley and McRaven and McMaster and John Kelly and serious people like that. He'd have my pillow guy and Mike Flynn and a rogues gallery of enablers and sycophants and loyalists.

nodes.speaker: Sam Harris
nodes.text: I'm pretty sure I don't want to live in a country where millions of children who are American citizens watch their parents get deported. If you just imagine what that would look like now, you might think, well, Trump's never going to do that.

nodes.speaker: Sam Harris
nodes.text: I mean, I think, Ben, if I had told you 10 years ago that you would one day support a presidential candidate who bragged about owning a teen beauty pageant so that he could barge into the dressing room and ogle naked underage girls. I don't think you would believe me.

nodes.speaker: Ben Shapiro
nodes.text: So again, you know, you talk about the peaceful transition of power, which actually did happen because Donald Trump did leave office in January of 2021. And Joe Biden has been president mostly for the last three and a half years.

nodes.speaker: Sam Harris
nodes.text: That was not. You cannot call January six a peaceful transfer of power.

nodes.speaker: Ben Shapiro
nodes.text: Well, that's not when the peaceful, that's not when the transfer of power happens, Sam. I mean, people don't leave office on January 6th. People leave office on January 20th.

nodes.speaker: Sam Harris
nodes.text: If Mike Pence had obeyed him, we would have had a genuine constitutional crisis.

nodes.speaker: Ben Shapiro
nodes.text: I agree with you, which is, I think that why Mike Pence did the right thing. And I think that what Donald Trump, the argument that Donald Trump was making between November and January was morally and legally specious. With that said, there was a peaceful transition of power.

nodes.speaker: Bari Weiss
nodes.text: But right now, on the campaign trail, when Donald Trump is asked, and he was on Joe Rogan a few days ago about the 2020 election, he still claims that he won the election. What do you make of that?

nodes.speaker: Ben Shapiro
nodes.text: I mean, I obviously disagree with him. I don't think that he won the 2020 election. Also, this is part and parcel. I wish it weren't. But it is part and parcel of a reactionary divide that has plagued our politics since before Donald Trump, frankly.

nodes.speaker: Ben Shapiro
nodes.text: When Donald Trump won the election in 2016, we then spent three and a half years of people claiming that Donald Trump was a Russian plant and had been bootstrapped into office by the Russians manipulating Facebook.

nodes.speaker: Sam Harris
nodes.text: It doesn't exist on both sides. That's not true.

nodes.speaker: Ben Shapiro
nodes.text: If Donald Trump wins this election, I promise you, you will see elections denialism on the left that is incredibly similar to what you've seen from Donald Trump circa 2020.

nodes.speaker: Sam Harris
nodes.text: I love that you said that. I wanted to ask you that. And this is a question for you. I'm not going to filibuster, Ben. I just want to ask you if on election day, we have a clear result, right?

nodes.speaker: Sam Harris
nodes.text: If I told you that the losing candidate denied the results of the election and simply would not accept a loss and even appealed to their most fanatical supporters to come into the streets and just obviously provoked violence on the part of the mob, right? Which candidate am I conceivably talking about?

nodes.speaker: Ben Shapiro
nodes.text: Well, I mean, I think that, you know, given Trump's rhetoric in 2020, obviously what you're talking about is Donald Trump. I don't think that's how it'd be phrased by Kamala Harris.

nodes.speaker: Ben Shapiro
nodes.text: The most plausible scenario is that she loses in a very tight election. And then she says there's been voter suppression. And then she said, it's all too plausible. Ben. It's all too plausible because currently the chance that there will be a landslide in the swing states is about 40%.

nodes.speaker: Ben Shapiro
nodes.text: Donald Trump has all. This is my original claim, Sam, is that Donald Trump is very obvious in his excesses. Democrats and the Democratic Party and Kamala Harris are much more. I would say, much more subtle in their excesses.

nodes.speaker: Sam Harris
nodes.text: Ben, you can't use a phrase like no less dangerous. Hillary Clinton conceded in 24 hours. That is less dangerous than this continuous provocation.

nodes.speaker: Ben Shapiro
nodes.text: Sam, I just go on for years. I just disagree with you. I think that the attempt by members of the media, by Hillary Clinton, who herself said that Donald Trump was illegitimately elected based on Russian interference in the 2016 election was highly damaging.

nodes.speaker: Sam Harris
nodes.text: No, no, it's. What. No, she says. What I. The same thing to say is that there is continuous foreign Interference in our elections. Right? That's all.

nodes.speaker: Ben Shapiro
nodes.text: That's the double standard, Sam. Now, now, now, proposing a double standard. No, no, but when Hillary says it, it's totally subtle and fine. And when Trump says it in the most obvious, foolish way, then it's totally different.

nodes.speaker: Sam Harris
nodes.text: Ben, you're just. You're just missing the relevant details here, that almost everyone thinks that there is continuous foreign interference in our elections. Very few people at this point think that it has been decisive and that it was decisive in 2016.

nodes.speaker: Sam Harris
nodes.text: What you do not have in the Democratic Party is a cult of personality around anyone and a series of loyalty tests. I mean, again, we're so far from the actual ground truth here.

nodes.speaker: Sam Harris
nodes.text: I mean, the ground truth is that we know at this point that there were Republicans in Congress who would have voted to impeach Trump after January 6th, but they didn't, because they were afraid for their lives.

nodes.speaker: Sam Harris
nodes.text: This is not normal, and it's not happening on the Democratic side. Right. This is a descent into, if you don't like the term fascism, something very much like fascism. I mean, I would say that this is fascism punching a gaping hole in our democracy, and it is the effect of Trump's demagoguery, his style of politics.
